speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we were-- oh, this is reel two of my interview with Robert Mendler. We were talking about your mother's family. Were there any other family members who you were close to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother had a brother. He was a doctor. He was a physician. He was my godfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Jacub, [? Kuba. ?] In Polish Jacub. He lives in-- in [INAUDIBLE]. He had a wonderful practice. It's very sad to talk about him, because my uncle married a Gentile girl after my-- after his father passed away. In Europe, you know how it was. Was very sad. The whole family disowned him. They say Kaddish like he wouldn't exist, like he were died because he went out of his faith. But when the war broke out, he was taken to the ghetto. He was very good-- very good doctor in the ghetto all through the war. Finally in 1942, when they were liquidating the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] his wife got him out of ghetto, smuggled him out, and she hid him all through the war underground. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Meantime, she was running around with his friends. They were Gentile friends. They were also doctors. But she kept him alive anyhow. The only contact I had with him before the war was myself with my uncle. When I come to visit my grandfather, which he didn't like her too well because my grandma's very religious, and it was embarrassing what he did. But I used to go to see him because he always used to give me $5. He used to treat me well. He was my uncle, my godfather. And during the war, same thing. I used to see him when he was in ghetto. But-- but finally when she him out of ghetto, she hid him underground all through the war. About two days before the Poland was liberated by the Russian, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she squeal on him. And they came in, the Gestapo. They arrest him and they killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned-- I got-- I received a letter and a picture of my uncle laying in a casket. They killed him and they let him be buried. I don't know where's he buried, honest to God really, but I have his picture. I got it from-- he was-- this fellow was right at this funeral. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I received a letter from this fellow in Poland with this picture and he run me all about it, what happened. I was very, very upset. He could've survived the war, all through the war. I don't know why she did it. I can't understand. Did you ever see her again afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never seen her. I never went back to Poland. But she used to correspond with my uncle here in United State which was his brother. He used to help out. She always used to ask him to send packages and this and that. Not only that, a fellow, my uncle went to school with him, the doctor in Vienna, Austria before the war. He happened to come to [INAUDIBLE] and took over his practice. And he saved all the equipment for him. He was a Austrian or a German fellow. He saved all the equipments, the whole practice for him. And the [? minute ?] were retreating the Nazis, he handed over the whole practice to his wife. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the war, she tried to sell. She wrote different kind of letters to my uncle about my uncle, that he was captured, all kind of baloneys. You know, I knew different story. And my uncle believed it. He didn't know what happened to him really till I start to correspond with him, and I tell him exactly what happened to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to send them money and packages, different kind of equipments so she could sell the equipment. She needed some kind of lenses, this and that. Just to help her out. When I find out about it, I made it stop. So unfortunate poor fellow, he went tragically. This was my mother brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other family members that you were close to during-- before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all were close with each other. In Europe the family was the family. For instance, a girl married and man brought her to the home to live with, like my father. My mother married my father. He came to live-- to Nowy Targ to live with my-- with my mother's family. The whole family was very, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you were growing up, were there any plans for your future?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always wanted be a doctor, I be honest with you. I want always take over to my uncle, you know? And I don't know what kind of plans my parents had for me. I always wanted come to America because I had a uncle here. You know, America was something for us. You know. And-- but actually I was just a youngster myself when I left home. I was only 13 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go at 13?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on my own when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me, did you have any contact with any refugees or anyone who was leaving Germany between 1933 and 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father family in Berlin. They live in Berlin. So 1938, some of the Jews, they were allowed to get out from Germany '37, '38, even early '39. So my father family cousins, they came over to Poland and they live in that town too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you know any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all rabbis.

speaker: interviewer
text: All rabbis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabbis, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did they resettle in your town in Nowy Targ?

speaker: interviewee
text: They resettled. There were family in [INAUDIBLE], and I had my uncle in [INAUDIBLE]. His name was [INAUDIBLE] in Vienna, Austria. They had beautiful hotel. It was very famous in Vienna, Austria. They had hotel and bakery. I have to tell you little bit something about my grandfather brother. Before the First World War, used to be Austo-Hungary, was Galicia then. And the-- the head of the government was Kaiser Franz Joseph. As much was anti-semitism, but he liked to have the adviser Jewish fellows, they used to be his advisers. So one of my grandfather brother, he was one of his adviser, Kaiser Franz Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what my-- my-- my mother used to tell me all about it, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when these relatives came from Berlin, what did they tell you what was going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had a chance to get out. They couldn't take so much out, so much money, not too much. They had to leave everything behind. They only-- they were allowed to take some. Some came with furnitures. They brought some furnitures. Some brought some clothes and things like that. They weren't allowed to take-- they weren't allowed to liquidate a property, nothing like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you about any of the laws or regulations?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- there-- I was just a child at home, but I was always interested. And they had always to complaining-- you know, the children weren't allowed to go to school. Their hard time when they came over because their children, they couldn't speak Polish. They speak only English.

speaker: interviewer
text: German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we could communicate with them, but ordinary people in that town, they couldn't communicate with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were speaking-- they were Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were speaking Germans, all right?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but they weren't speaking English. You had said--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, just wondering.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no English language then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- so then you had this whole group of cousins that came in and did they go to school with you and try-- you know, did you try to teach them Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we tried to teach them. They went to cheder with us. And the German language, it's almost-- it's like-- like a Jewish language. We called [INAUDIBLE], Jewish language is [INAUDIBLE]. It's similar to German language. If you can speak Yiddish, you could communicate yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Same thing during the war too with the-- with the Nazis. You know, you could communicate with-- a lot-- a lot of Jewish people, that couldn't speak German again, but they spoke Yiddish. But still they could communicate with each other. So for them, like my cousins, you know, they could communicate with us, you know? But not with public. Were they as religious as your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how were they-- how did they fit in with the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a tough time. They live only for short time with us, then they moved to Krakow, you know, the largest city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess they were more comfortable. Their parents, they observed Shabbat too, Shabbat like everybody else, but not the way like we did. For instant in our home, Shabbat was Shabbat, you know? At the dinner used to sit. You know, we weren't allowed to leave the table. Used to sing [INAUDIBLE]. You know, we were very-- like there was no such a thing conservative or reform. Everybody was Orthodox. We're Orthodox. There were Hasidim and there were Orthodox. There were Orthodox people. They used to shave their beard. Nobody wore a beard. My father didn't wear no beard for instance. He was a businessman, was-- it wasn't proper for him to wear a beard, really. Because they were a lot of Jewish people that are Hasidic and they were very good businessmen. They used to-- didn't mind. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, during the summer of 1939 before the war, before the Germans invaded Poland, were there any warnings? Any signs? Any rumors? Did you have a feeling something was going to happen or not happen or-- what was the mood in your family? We knew something was brewing but nothing like that. Nobody expect war going to broke out because Hitler was the man he want to build-- he wanted corridor to Danzig. That's where the whole thing started, you know? After he took Czechoslovakia and Slovenia and-- and Austria so he wanted corridor to Danzig because Danzig was a free city, was actually on the Polish protectorate and it was German too. It spoke Polish and German. But there was-- there were some sign because the special militia prepared people in case there's attack or something. Well, the government actually knew what was happening, but ordinary people-- don't forget, we had no TV. There was no TV, very few radios. In our home, I remember we had the crystal radio with the earphones, you know? There was no radio then.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember our home we were-- as a matter of fact, before we had electricity which is the carbon lights. I live actually in 19th century and in the 20th century.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you reading newspapers at that age at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Polish newspaper and Jewish newspaper.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were aware of the invasions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because I was already-- don't forget, I was already around 12 years old. I was in school already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you on September 1, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: On September 1, 1930-- we were all at home. We didn't expect-- the war broke out 5 o'clock in the morning and our town was located only like 14 kilometers from the Czechoslovakian border. By then, the Czechoslovakia was already occupied by the German. So in no time, around 2 o'clock afternoon, the German were already in our town. The Polish Army, they were running away. There was no motorized vehicles, only on foot and or horses. They were running. They were leaving everything behind. They were running away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when there were German coming, so my father escape. He ran away and I got-- me and my brother and my mother stay home. Where did your father go?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father went on the Russian side because Russia also got into-- Russia occupy half of the Poland and Germany, they had pact between each other and Germany occupy the other half. So my father went on the Russian side. I wish he would've stayed, then maybe he would have been alive, who knows?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why he went to the Russian side?

speaker: interviewee
text: He followed the others, I suppose. But he was lucky thing for him because when the German came to our town, right away they came in the-- with the list. They were searching for Jewish hostages and had my father name on the list. I don't know how they got that. You know, they came in-- and they-- they took right away Jewish hostages. I remember they came over, the Germans, to our town. I was just a youngster, and I wasn't afraid. You know, my mother, she didn't like it. She was don't go, don't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were lived only about a half a block from our square, we had beautiful square around town. And I remember the German came in from all four side with the tanks. So I went, and-- and our square, in the center of the square was a-- they called it 'ratusz.' Was a-- what should I say? It's a city hall. So the German came in and, you know, to me was like a novelty to see a German. I didn't know who they were and so forth on. And I remember I ran to the city hall with friend of mine in Israel, he lives in Israel. He was a policeman.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was sitting drunk, and the German officer talked to him in Polish, we take over the town. And he was demanding right away with the list. He came in where the people live. So he walk around with them, show where the Jewish people lived. Right away they took Jewish hostages and some of the prominent people, whoever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a list already prepared of Jewish names?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were Jewish names, yeah. They came to our house. They were looking for my father because my father wasn't home. This was a few hours after the occupation, maybe the next day I believe, yeah. My father wasn't home.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother say, he's not home. Where is he? Where is he hiding? My mother said he's not hiding. He went with-- he ran away with the other people. She doesn't know herself where he is which because we didn't know where he was either till we find out, till we got later on message.

speaker: interviewee
text: People-- some people came back from the Russian zone, you know, and they tell us that my father survive. He's in Lvov-- Limberg, they call it. But after awhile, few months later when things quiet down so my father return, he came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me approximately what month he came back? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came back [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about two months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: In November?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, about two months later. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. In the meantime, after the German occupation of Nowy Targ, can you tell me what changed in Nowy Targ?

speaker: interviewee
text: What changed right away, you know, I remember they start to right away after the Jewish people. We weren't allowed to go to school. This was the first thing. Jewish kids weren't allowed to go to school with a proper education. We had to go to work. The first job is to sweep up the streets, the sidewalks. Women used to-- like my mother, they took her to work. She used to clean the offices, scrub the floors, windows, sidewalks with a brush. You know, they were taking all kind of pictures. They were cutting the beards from the Jew because we had a lot of Hasidim in our town. Don't forget, our town was 11,000 population, there were 3,000 Jews-- 30%-- and were a lot of Hasidim. Was the majority of our town were Hasidim really.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the relations between the Jews and the Gentiles before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't too bad, you know? There was always anti-semitism. They used to call you names and oh, and that. You know, they used to break the windows. They used to beat you up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that change after the German occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was worse. Was worse because the German did it, the same thing and worse. And they would use a lot of the Pollacks. If they were looking for somebody, they would use a lot of the Pollack. They give them extra ration so forth on.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't pleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had-- did your father do any military service during his life?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was soldier First World War. He wasn't drafted when the Second World War broke out. They-- they-- they draft some Polish people before the war, see the sign was there. They mobilized, but they mobilized about two, three days before the German occupy our town. You know? So my father didn't go to the army because he had a family at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did he fight for in the First World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Austro-Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it make any difference that he had fought for Austro-Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't make any different at all. My father as a matter of fact had a-- he was in the cavalry in the horses, and he used to-- I remember we had a picture of him. He used to have a big saber, and he had a scar here on his face. He was-- when he fought in the First World War he was hit with a saber on his cheek. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we always had pictures hanging on the wall. But didn't mean anything to them. He had decoration from the war, from bravery. Didn't mean a thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he ran away, do you know how he got out of town? Did he walk or?

speaker: interviewee
text: He walk, [INAUDIBLE]. He walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did he take many things with him or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just took away with nothing. Whatever-- food, stuff like that. Nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about the regulations and the changes in Nowy Targ after the German occupation. You said there was an increase in anti-semitism and that you weren't allowed to go to school. What other changes were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were, like I said, we were deprived our education and so forth on. Different was we start to go to work. We become right away a slave. My first job for instance-- I was just 13 years old. My first job was I was assigned to the chief of the Gestapo. I knew this fellow before the war. He become a chief. He must've been a spy or something. He lived in our town before the war, never used the language-- German. He spoke perfectly Polish. Later in the program, I come back what happened to him. When the German came in, he become a-- we used to call him [GERMAN]. He used to wear the party-- he was a party man. Hakenkreuz he used to wear on his lapel. And he knew my family because used to come to our house, used to drink the soda water. See, we had a big porch in our house and people-- we had a table. People used to come down-- beside what we sold to the stores, people used to come down and buy a bottle of seltzer and sit down and drink. The Monsignor from the church, he used to come every day to our house. He were very good friend of my dad, and they used to drink the water too. And he, this guy, this fellow, he used to be organist in the church. His name was Roman like my name. Polish, I'm Roman, but his last name was Roman. Nobody, I mean, thought about things like that. When the German came in he became was a chief of the Gestapo. He knew exactly every-- he knew all the Jews in town. That's why I think he must've give them all the information, the prominent Jews in town, because they came-- the-- there was-- they took the rabbi and all the prominent Jews, whoever could count as hostages. Nobody-- there's only one fellow that returned back from-- from the camp. They took him right Germany, but the rest of them were all killed I suppose. And what happened, my first job I used to work for him. He picked me up to work for him because he knew my family. And I-- I thought I-- I was pretty happy because we knew each other. I thought he going to be good for me-- to me. Meantime this man, he became such a sadist. I was his house boy. I work for him. He used to beat the heck out of me every day. I used to come home after work to cry. I complained to my mother-- I was 13 years old [INAUDIBLE] with a bloody nose. My eyes were bloodshot, my ears-- pick me up-- pick me up by the ears just to punish me for no reason. I couldn't say nothing. I used to cry to my [INAUDIBLE]. My mother always encouraged me. There's nothing I can do. Let's hope the war be over pretty soon. I can see how she felt, how I would feel if something would happen to my child but her hands were tied. I could never forget this man, never. He put a-- he put a mark on me because my father never punished us by hitting or something like that. He was the first man put a hand on me really. Broke my nose. And I could never forget the man even during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, all those years where I spent in camp you loose faith, let's face it. I'm not the only one I'm sure. Majority of us, how could there be God? Where is the God? What happens to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: But always I thought to myself, I was praying every night before I went to sleep if I-- and I had such a tremendous will to live. I want to live. I want to live so bad. I don't want to die. I don't want to become a Muslim or something like that. I want to find this man someday-- come later on to the story what happened to me, coming to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I work for him for about-- about four months maybe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I was assigned to different-- in 1940 I was assigned to work in a stone quarry in a small town not to far from my hometown called Zakopane. Zakopane is resort town, a small town. 1938 it were the Winter Olympics in this little town, Zakopane.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, when we were children, when we were kids at home, we used to hike, you know, from-- from the Zionist organization. Early in the morning we start to hike from our hometown to Zakopane. The next day, my uncle-- I had-- I had relatives in Zakopane [INAUDIBLE] used to bring us back home. So I work-- I was assigned to work in the stone quarry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned before that you were very-- your family was good friends with the Monsignor. Was there anyone prominent in the town who could have helped you or tried to help you in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. There were quite a few because don't forget we were in business, and we'd been in business for a long time. Everybody knew my family, especially my mother's side family, Mendlers. We were very famous in town, you know, not because we weren't millionaires. We weren't rich but we were a little bit better off than some others maybe. And everybody knew Mendlers and the family. And there were a lot of Gentile people, they could have saved-- they could've help us. I have to tell you after a while what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop it right there.
